title: GC.SuppressFinalize should not be invoked for types without destructors
description: "GC.SuppressFinalize" should not be invoked for types without destructorsAnalyze your codeintentionality-logicalmaintainabilityCode SmellQuick FixIDE quick fixes available with SonarQube for IDEunusedconfusingWhy is this an issue?GC.SuppressFinalizeasks the Common Language Runtime not to call the finalizer of an object. This is useful when implementing the dispose pattern where object finalization is already handled inIDisposable.Dispose. However, it has no effect if there is no finalizer defined in the object’s type, so using it in such cases is just confusing.This rule raises an issue whenGC.SuppressFinalizeis called for objects ofsealedtypes without a finalizer.Note:S3971is a stricter version of this rule.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

logic.detection_conditions: Detects GC.SuppressFinalize in classes without finalizers (destructors)
logic.triggering_conditions: Triggers when: Class contains GC.SuppressFinalize but no destructor (~ClassName)
logic.exclusion_conditions: Excludes: classes with destructors, comments, test code
logic.reasoning: GC.SuppressFinalize is only useful for types with finalizers/destructors. Calling it for types without destructors is confusing and serves no purpose. This rule detects classes containing 'GC.SuppressFinalize' but no destructor syntax ('~'). Pattern matches GC.SuppressFinalize while excluding class sources that contain '~' followed by identifier (destructor syntax).

should_trigger: public sealed class MyClass : IDisposable { public void Dispose() { GC.SuppressFinalize(this); } }
should_not_trigger: public sealed class MyClass : IDisposable { public void Dispose() { GC.SuppressFinalize(this); } ~MyClass() { } }
public sealed class MyClass : IDisposable { public void Dispose() { } }
